%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Example: Butterworth Filter Transformation

> **Section Objective:** Transform a continuous-time Butterworth lowpass filter into discrete-time filters and compare which method preserves the cutoff best.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- 10th-order Butterworth prototype
- sampling-period setup
- FOBD transformation
- bilinear transformation
- prewarped bilinear transformation
- magnitude-response comparison

## 1. Start from the Continuous-Time Butterworth Prototype

This example begins with a **continuous-time 10th-order Butterworth lowpass filter**. Before any discrete-time transformation is applied, MATLAB computes the filter's poles and polynomial denominator coefficients using the `roots` and `poly` commands.

The analog prototype is the starting point — a well-defined continuous-time filter whose frequency response we want to approximate in discrete time.

Here, the cutoff is 3000 Hz, so the continuous-time cutoff angular frequency is written as \(\omega_c = 2\pi\cdot3000\).

#### Note
This page is about **preparing the analog prototype only**. The comparison between digital transformation methods comes later. Do not skip this step — the quality of all three digital filters depends on correctly specifying the analog prototype.

## 2. Set the digital frequency scale before transforming

- \(\omega_c\) is the **continuous-time cutoff angular frequency** in rad/s.
- The factor \(2\pi\) converts cycles per second (Hz) to radians per second — this is always required when a cutoff is given in Hz but the transformation code expects angular frequency.
- Use this value when setting up the analog prototype before any analog-to-digital transformation.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Do not treat 3000 as already being in rad/s. The raw frequency in Hz must always be multiplied by \(2\pi\) to obtain \(\omega_c\).

$$\omega_c = 2\pi\cdot3000$$

## 3. Compare the three transformation methods

The sampling rate in this example is **10 kHz**, so the sampling period is \(T = 1/10000\) seconds per sample.

Once \(T\) is known, the **desired digital cutoff frequency** is computed as \(\Omega_c = \omega_c T\). This tells us exactly where the cutoff should appear on the digital frequency axis, which runs from \(0\) to \(\pi\) radians/sample.

Numerical check: the cutoff is 3000 Hz and the sampling rate is 10000 Hz, so the ratio is 0.3 — the digital cutoff is below \(\pi\) but not negligibly small. This means frequency warping effects will be visible and matter.

### EXAM TRIGGER
Whenever a problem gives an **analog cutoff in Hz** and a **sampling rate**, convert the scale using \(\Omega_c = \omega_c T\) before judging where the digital cutoff should appear on the response plot.

$$T = \frac{1}{10000}$$
*\(T\) is the **sampling period** in seconds, equal to the reciprocal of the sampling rate.

- Use \(T\) before applying any analog-to-digital filter transformation.
- It appears in the digital cutoff formula \(\Omega_c = \omega_c T\) and in the prewarping parameter.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Do not pass the sampling rate (10000) where the period \(T\) is required. The period is \(1/10000\), not \(10000\).*

$$\Omega_c = \omega_c T$$
*\(\Omega_c\) is the **desired digital cutoff frequency** in radians/sample.

- \(\omega_c\) is the continuous-time cutoff in rad/s.
- \(T\) is the sampling period in seconds/sample.
- Together, \(\omega_c T\) maps the analog cutoff onto the \(0\) to \(\pi\) digital frequency axis.
- This is the reference line used to judge whether each transformation method places the cutoff correctly.

#### WARNING
Confusing lowercase \(\omega\) (continuous-time, rad/s) with uppercase \(\Omega\) (discrete-time, rad/sample) causes cutoff-location errors. They are related by \(T\), not interchangeable.*

## 3. Compare the Three Transformation Methods

With the analog prototype and digital frequency scale established, three MATLAB-based transformations are applied and compared:

- **FOBD (First-Order Backward Difference):** Applied via `CH5MP3`. This method maps the analog filter to a digital filter but introduces **significant cutoff-frequency distortion**. The resulting filter is lowpass in shape, but the cutoff does not land where the design requires.
- **Ordinary Bilinear Transformation (BLT):** Applied via `CH5MP4` with the standard sampling period \(T\). This is better than FOBD — the response is closer to ideal — but the inherent frequency warping of the bilinear map causes the cutoff to **fall short** of the desired location.
- **Prewarped Bilinear Transformation:** Applied via `CH5MP4` with a **modified period** \(T_{\text{prewarp}}\). This compensates for the bilinear warping at the chosen cutoff frequency, so the cutoff **lands correctly**.

#### NEAR-MISS WARNING
The OCR source uses the label 'First-order backward difference transformation' in the code comments. Follow the plotted legend and surrounding explanation, which consistently use **FOBD = first-order backward difference**. Do not confuse this with forward difference.

$$T_{\text{prewarp}} = \frac{2}{\omega_c}\tan\left(\frac{\Omega_c}{2}\right)$$
*This is the **modified period parameter** passed into the bilinear transformation function when prewarping is applied.

- \(\omega_c\): the desired analog cutoff frequency in rad/s.
- \(\Omega_c\): the desired digital cutoff frequency in rad/sample.
- The tangent function compensates for the nonlinear frequency compression that ordinary BLT introduces.
- **When to use it:** whenever ordinary BLT would warp the cutoff away from the target frequency and you need the cutoff to land precisely.

#### COMMON MISUSE
Do not apply prewarping without first identifying the specific frequency that must be preserved. Prewarping corrects the response at **one chosen frequency** — it is not a general improvement across all frequencies.*


%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Analog cutoff: \(\omega_c = 2\pi\cdot3000\) rad/s — always multiply Hz by \(2\pi\) before using in filter code.
- Sampling period: \(T = 1/10000\) s — pass the period, not the rate, into transformation functions.
- Digital cutoff: \(\Omega_c = \omega_c T\) rad/sample — this is the target location on the \(0\) to \(\pi\) axis.
- Prewarping parameter: \(T_{\text{prewarp}} = \frac{2}{\omega_c}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_c}{2}\right)\) — FOBD distorts cutoff badly, ordinary BLT falls short, prewarped BLT locates the cutoff correctly.

*Next, we will see why cascaded second-order sections improve high-order IIR filter implementation.*
